tossups.question: Translation or original language term acceptable. Extensive cleaning was required when this system was used to facilitate the daimyō-gyōretsu. Using this system was the most common reason an official or noble would use a honjin, in contrast with commoners using hatago. The names of Kansai and Kanto are derived from being west or east of a place used by this system in Hakone (“ha-koh-nay”), which facilitated the elaborate processions of the Sankin-kōtai. During the 17th century, cedar and cryptomeria trees were planted as part of the establishment of this system, whose ceremonial center was the Nihonbashi. (*) Tokugawa Ieyasu established 53 locations called shukuba as part of this system, whose “Central Mountain” and “East Sea” sections are depicted in two different series of ukiyo-e prints by Utagawa Hiroshige. For 10 points, scenic post stations were built to provide food and shelter to users of what transportation system designed to converge in Tokyo?
tossups.question_sanitized: Translation or original language term acceptable. Extensive cleaning was required when this system was used to facilitate the daimyo-gyoretsu. Using this system was the most common reason an official or noble would use a honjin, in contrast with commoners using hatago. The names of Kansai and Kanto are derived from being west or east of a place used by this system in Hakone ("ha-koh-nay"), which facilitated the elaborate processions of the Sankin-kotai. During the 17th century, cedar and cryptomeria trees were planted as part of the establishment of this system, whose ceremonial center was the Nihonbashi. (*) Tokugawa Ieyasu established 53 locations called shukuba as part of this system, whose "Central Mountain" and "East Sea" sections are depicted in two different series of ukiyo-e prints by Utagawa Hiroshige. For 10 points, scenic post stations were built to provide food and shelter to users of what transportation system designed to converge in Tokyo?
tossups.answer: Gokaidō [or the Five Routes; accept Tōkaidō or Nakasendō or Kōshū Kaidō or Ōshū Kaidō or Nikkō Kaidō; accept East Sea Route or Central Mountain Route until read; prompt on answers describing the Japanese or Tokugawa system of roads or highway; prompt on post stations until mentioned and ask “what larger system were these built around”]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Gokaido [or the Five Routes; accept Tokaido or Nakasendo or Koshu Kaido or Oshu Kaido or Nikko Kaido; accept East Sea Route or Central Mountain Route until read; prompt on answers describing the Japanese or Tokugawa system of roads or highway; prompt on post stations until mentioned and ask "what larger system were these built around"]

set.name: 2024 History of the World Part II

tossups.question: Three boats are seen without sails on the far left of one depiction of this entity, and four more ships sail behind a row of merchant’s booths in that work. A man in a yellow hat appears to forcefully grab at a woman in a depiction immediately following that work. One depiction of this object includes three people walking in the snow with the person on the left appearing to have a cane and an umbrella. The final depiction of this object features carts and travelers crossing a river as they arrive at the imperial capital. Those works comprise a series that begins on the Nihonbashi and includes depictions of Kanagawa and Kambara. For 10 points, name this coastal road between Kyoto and Tokyo, depicted in a series of fifty three ukiyo-e prints by Utagawa Hiroshige.

bonuses.leadin: A giant skeleton summoned by Takiyasha the witch takes up two panels of a triptych by this artist. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: A giant skeleton summoned by Takiyasha the witch takes up two panels of a triptych by this artist. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this Japanese artist who depicted the generals of Takeda Shingen in his 24 Generals of Kai Province.
Kuniyoshi work<u>ed in ukiyo</u>-e, a genre that uses these objects to create prints. These objects are carved, soaked in ink, and then pressed into cloth.
In a uk<u>iyo-e series</u>, Uta<u>gawa Hir</u>oshige depicted 53 of these places located along the Tōkaidō road. In another series, he depicted 69 of these places on the Kiso Kaidō.
parts_sanitized: Name this Japanese artist who depicted the generals of Takeda Shingen in his 24 Generals of Kai Province.
Kuniyoshi worked in ukiyo-e, a genre that uses these objects to create prints. These objects are carved, soaked in ink, and then pressed into cloth.
In a ukiyo-e series, Utagawa Hiroshige depicted 53 of these places located along the Tokaido road. In another series, he depicted 69 of these places on the Kiso Kaido.
answers_sanitized: post stations [or 53 Stations of the Tokaido]

bonuses.leadin: For 10 points each, answer some questions about the artistic inspiration of Szymborska’s poetry.
parts: This poetry collection by Szymborska was inspired by a woodblock print created by Utagawa Hiroshige, and this collection itself was the inspiration for an experimental film written and directed by Beata (bay-ah-tah) Pozniak. The title poem of this collection begins with the speaker stating that this is an “odd planet, and those on it are odd too.”

In a short Szymborska poem, the speaker states that the “World does not deserve” to end as long as a woman in a painting by this artist keeps pouring milk “from the jug into the bowl” “day after day” in “silence and concentration.”
answers: The People on the Bridge [or Ludzie na moście]
answers_sanitized: The People on the Bridge [or Ludzie na moscie]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: For 10 points each, answer some questions about the artistic inspiration of Szymborska's poetry.

In a short Szymborska poem, the speaker states that the "World does not deserve" to end as long as a woman in a painting by this artist keeps pouring milk "from the jug into the bowl" "day after day" in "silence and concentration."
